column1: Anuttara: The Absolute, beyond which nothing exists
Paramaśiva: Supreme consciousness as unified reality
Prakāśa: Light of consciousness, self-illuminating awareness
Cicchakti: Power of consciousness to manifest reality
Spanda: Divine vibration or pulsation of consciousness
Ābhāsavāda: Doctrine of manifestation as appearance
36 Tattvas: Principles of manifestation from Śiva to Earth
Māyā: Creative power that generates differentiation
Trika: Three-fold division: Śiva, Śakti, Nara (individual)
Five Acts (Pañcakṛtya): Creation, maintenance, dissolution, concealment, grace
Pratyabhijñā: Recognition of one's identity with Śiva
Kaula: Embodied practice integrating all aspects of experience
Kula: Total integration of reality in consciousness
Spanda Texts: Focusing on divine vibration
Pratyabhijñā Texts: Focusing on recognition philosophy
Krama Texts: Focusing on sequential realization
Kula Texts: Focusing on embodied totality
Mālinīvijayottara Tantra: Primary source text for Trika
Svacchanda Tantra: Important for ritual elements
Netra Tantra: Focuses on protective practices and subtle anatomy
Rudrayāmala Tantra: Early source for Trika-Kaula synthesis
Parātriṃśikā: Concise revelation on supreme reality
Śiva Sūtras: Revealed aphorisms attributed to Śiva via Vasugupta
Spandakārikā: Verses on divine vibration by Vasugupta/Kallata
Śivadṛṣṭi: Somānanda's foundational text on divine perception
Pratyabhijñāhṛdaya: Kṣemarāja's concise summary in 20 sutras
